description: The firm fibrous material in the stems and roots of trees and other woody plants is 'wolea'. The word also covers that material after cutting and drying. 'shiro' (tree) names the living growth form, while wood names the substance a sentence handles as material.
articulatory_notes: Rounded lips carry voiced 'w' directly into back 'o'. Lateral 'l' opens onto stressed front 'e', followed by a clear hiatus into final open 'a'.
usage_notes: Species, grain, moisture, and treatment describe the board in hand. Provenance or structural grade can join the account when the work depends on them.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A board still answers to the tree's grain and the water it has lost. Sawing, joining, and repair go better when the worker reads those traces instead of treating every board as interchangeable.

semantic_domains.creation: plant material worked into structures and objects or remade as fuel and paper
semantic_domains.nature: fibrous structural tissue produced by woody plants
semantic_domains.physical: a firm cellular material whose behaviour follows grain and moisture
